div.head: ES–32. Memorandum of Discussion at the 466th Meeting of the National Security CouncilSource: Eisenhower Library, Whitman File, NSC Records. Top Secret. Eyes Only. This memorandum was prepared by Robert H. Johnson, Director of the NSC Planning Board Secretariat, on November 8, 1960. The source text contains a list of 27 attendees.
div.p: [Here follows discussion of agenda items 1–4, concerning outer space programs, Cuba, significant world events affecting U.S. security (Sino-Soviet clash, Laos El Salvador), and Algeria. Portions printed below are from the merged discussion of items 3 and 4.]

Dealing next with El Salvador, General Cabell said that the Communists and pro-Castro forces were making a strong bid for power. Ex-President Osorio, who had used these forces as a means of getting back to power, had appointed pro-Communists and pro-Castro individuals to the Cabinet and other posts. Osorio thought he could control these elements but they have moved quickly to reinforce their position. They have appointed Communists and pro-Communists to second and third echelon positions in the government and were moving in on information and propaganda media. They are also demanding removal of all Osorio’s supporters. Osorio hopes to rule El Salvador indirectly until the 1952 elections. He says that he cannot consider plans to restrict the extreme Left because he needs their support to get action on certain reform measures. The army was apprehensive and a group of officers had plans for salvaging the dominant position of the military. They must act quickly, however, if their action is to be effective. General Cabell suggested that control of El Salvador by pro-Castro elements would pose a series threat to Guatemala.
[Here follows discussion of possible Soviet intervention in Algeria.]

Secretary Merchant went on to point out that the U.S. had not yet recognized the new government in El Salvador although we are considering the matter on a 24-hour basis. We had thought it unwise to recognize the government in view of the possibility of a counter-coup by conservative elements in the army. This was a difficult choice. The question was whether recognition would strengthen the reasonable elements in the government despite the fact that Leftist elements were honeycombing the government. Secretary Merchant expressed the view that Osorio may well have been double-crossed. He said that a decision would be needed within the next 24–72 hours. If we waited too long and there was no counter-coup, we would lose our ability to influence those who are our friends in the existing government.

The President observed that it was frightening how a few Communist elements could get in and take over a government while the others in the government behaved like jellyfish. It was also alarming that we could not get the OAS to be really concerned about such a situation because of the political problems that these governments have at home. He observed that we might have to go back to greater reliance on power politics.

Mr. Mann stated that a decision on recognition had to be made on the basis of an estimate of whether the existing El Salvador Government would be around long enough to make support worthwhile or whether we should work for a counter-revolution. The President suggested that we ought to find out whether the Rightists in El Salvador believe that it would be useful for us to recognize the regime or whether they consider that withholding recognition would be helpful in stimulating a counter-coup. Secretary Merchant observed that U.S. recognition could dishearten the opposition. The President asked whether we were talking to the opposition. Mr. Mann said that we were and that a majority of our friends in the governments were urging prompt recognition. However, he also acknowledged that friendly army officers took the opposite view. Secretary
Anderson said that he did not see how could run a government with the second and third echelons in the control of the Communists and the top of the government split; he did not see how any government could long exist on this basis on our side. Secretary Gates expressed the view that we should drag our feet on recognition because recognition would create a UN problem; that is, it would provide a basis for possible later UN intervention. He felt we were entitled to a period of time for foot dragging. The President stated that we did not want to let the situation sit and simmer; we needed to decide the best way to achieve our objectives. Secretary Merchant concluded this portion of the discussion by observing that there was no doubt that Osorio was friendly to us; the only question was whether he could control the situation.

3. Noted and discussed an oral briefing by the Acting Director of Central Intelligence on the subject, with specific reference to Sino-Soviet relations; the situation with respect to Algeria; and the situations in Laos and El Salvador.This paragraph constitutes NSC Action 2330, approved by the President on November 10, 1960. (S/S–NSC (Miscellaneous) Files, Lot 66 D 95, NSC Records of Action)
[Here follow the NSC Action on Algeria and discussion and Action on agenda item 5, Scandinavia.]
